enrichment.short_description: Knock is a notification infrastructure platform that gives engineering teams APIs and a workflow builder to send email, SMS, push, chat, and in-app notifications.
enrichment.seo_title: Knock Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Knock is a notification infrastructure API for developers. See how Knock's workflows, pricing tiers, and features compare to Courier, Novu, and OneSignal.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Knock is a notification infrastructure platform, sold as a hosted API and workflow engine, that lets product and engineering teams add multi-channel notifications, in-app inboxes, and customer messaging to their applications without building delivery logic from scratch. The company was founded in January 2021 by Sam Seely and Chris Bell, who had previously worked together as product and engineering leaders at Frame.io, where they experienced first-hand how painful it is to build and maintain notification systems as an application scales across email, SMS, push, and in-app channels. Knock is headquartered in Brooklyn, New York. The company raised a previously undisclosed 6 million dollar seed round led by Afore Capital, followed by a 12 million dollar Series A in February 2024 led by Craft Ventures with participation from Afore Capital and Preface Capital, bringing total disclosed funding to roughly 18 million dollars. Architecturally, Knock sits between an application's backend and its various delivery providers (Twilio, SendGrid, Postmark, Slack, and similar services): developers define reusable notification workflows, multi-step, multi-channel messages with conditional branches, delays, batching and digest logic, and per-recipient preference checks, either through a visual workflow builder in the Knock dashboard or in code, then trigger those workflows from application events or webhooks. Knock ingests recipient and object data from data warehouses, CDPs, reverse-ETL tools, and direct API or webhook calls, and lets teams model custom objects so that notification templates can be personalized dynamically at send time. The platform ships official SDKs for eight backend languages (Node, Python, Ruby, Go, Java, .NET, Elixir, and PHP), plus frontend components for building notification feeds and inboxes, a CLI that integrates with AI coding tools such as Cursor and Claude Code, environment-based version control with rollback for workflows, and built-in observability so teams can trace and debug individual message deliveries. Newer additions include an AI-assisted workflow builder that can draft a multi-step messaging journey from a single prompt, AI-generated and brand-customized email templates, and 'guides', targeted in-app messages such as banners, modals, and tooltips, which are also usage-metered. Knock reports processing hundreds of millions of messages per month for more than 200 companies, including Vercel, Webflow, Medium, Clay, Discogs, and Amplitude, and advertises 99.99 percent uptime along with HIPAA, SOC 2, GDPR, and CCPA compliance. Pricing follows a three-tier, usage-metered model. The free Developer plan costs 0 dollars per month and includes 10,000 messages, 500 monthly active guide users, and 500 AI agent credits, alongside unlimited workflows, broadcasts, channels, team members, and recipients. The Starter plan costs 250 dollars per month and raises the included volume to 50,000 messages and 2,500 guide active users at lower per-unit overage rates, and adds the ability to remove Knock branding from emails plus email client preview testing. The Enterprise tier uses custom, volume-based pricing and unlocks per-customer branding and preferences, translation and internationalization support, HIPAA compliance with a signed BAA, SAML 2.0 single sign-on, and a shared Slack channel with priority support. Usage-based overage charges are billed in arrears on the following month's invoice across all tiers. Knock is aimed primarily at software engineering and product teams at B2B SaaS and marketplace companies that need to ship reliable, cross-channel notifications quickly rather than build and maintain their own delivery, templating, preference-management, and retry infrastructure in-house; it is less suited to marketers running large-scale campaigns without engineering involvement, since Knock is fundamentally a developer-first, API-driven platform rather than a drag-and-drop marketing automation tool. Its closest direct competitors are Courier, another developer-focused notification-infrastructure API with similar workflow concepts, and Novu, an open-source notification infrastructure project that companies can self-host as a free alternative to Knock's hosted service. Knock also competes at the edges with broader customer engagement platforms such as Braze and Customer.io, which offer notification sending as part of much larger campaign and analytics suites aimed at marketing teams, and with raw delivery providers like Twilio, SendGrid, and OneSignal, which handle transport for a single channel but leave workflow orchestration and preferences for the customer to build. Knock's differentiation rests on combining a hosted, reliability-focused delivery layer with a developer-friendly workflow and data model, version control, and observability tooling purpose-built for engineering teams shipping notification features inside a product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Knock is a notification infrastructure platform founded in 2021 by former Frame.io product and engineering leaders Sam Seely and Chris Bell. Rather than a marketing campaign tool, Knock is a developer-first API and workflow engine that sits between an application's backend and its delivery providers, handling the plumbing of sending reliable, multi-channel notifications.

Teams define reusable notification workflows in a visual builder or in code, covering email, SMS, push, in-app messages, chat, and Slack, with conditional logic, batching, and digesting built in. Knock is backed by Craft Ventures and Afore Capital and is used in production by companies including Vercel, Webflow, and Amplitude.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Knock's core is the workflow engine: multi-step, multi-channel messaging journeys with per-recipient preference checks, send-window controls, and an AI workflow builder that can draft a workflow from a single prompt. Data can be synced in from warehouses, CDPs, or webhooks to personalize templates dynamically.

For developers, Knock offers SDKs in eight languages, a CLI compatible with AI coding assistants, environment-based version control with rollback, and built-in observability for tracing individual message deliveries. Prebuilt frontend components add in-app notification feeds, inboxes, and targeted guides like banners and modals.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Knock uses a three-tier, usage-metered pricing model. The free Developer plan includes 10,000 messages and 500 guide active users per month with unlimited workflows and channels. The Starter plan costs 250 dollars per month for 50,000 messages and adds branding removal and email previews.

Enterprise pricing is custom and volume-based, unlocking translation support, HIPAA BAAs, SAML SSO, and priority support. Overage charges across all tiers are billed in arrears on the following month's invoice.

features.title: Cross-channel workflows
features.description: Build reusable multi-step notification workflows that send across email, SMS, push, chat, and in-app channels with conditional branching, delays, and batching logic.

features.title: AI workflow builder
features.description: Generate a complete multi-channel messaging workflow from a single natural-language prompt, then refine the steps in the visual builder or in code.

features.title: In-app inbox and guides
features.description: Drop in prebuilt frontend components for notification feeds, inboxes, and targeted in-app guides like banners, modals, and tooltips.

features.title: Preference and send-window controls
features.description: Give end users self-serve control over which notification types and channels they receive, plus quiet-hours and digest scheduling.

features.title: Data ingestion and personalization
features.description: Sync recipient, object, and event data from warehouses, CDPs, reverse ETL tools, or webhooks to personalize templates at send time.

features.title: Developer tooling
features.description: Ship notifications with SDKs for eight languages, a CLI that works with AI coding assistants, environment-based version control, and CI validation.

features.title: Observability and debugging
features.description: Trace individual message deliveries, inspect logs, and roll back workflow versions when something goes wrong in production.

features.title: Enterprise security and compliance
features.description: Meet regulatory requirements with HIPAA BAAs, SOC 2 compliance, GDPR/CCPA support, and SAML 2.0 SSO on enterprise plans.

pros: Generous free tier with 10,000 messages per month and unlimited workflows and channels
Purpose-built for engineering teams, with SDKs, CLI, and version-controlled workflows
Supports a wide range of channels including email, SMS, push, chat, and Slack from one workflow
Strong observability and debugging tools for tracing individual notification deliveries
Backed by well-known customers such as Vercel, Webflow, and Amplitude, suggesting production reliability at scale
cons: Usage-based pricing means costs can climb quickly for high-volume senders
Enterprise features like SAML SSO, i18n, and HIPAA BAAs are locked behind custom pricing
Requires engineering resources to implement; not a self-serve tool for marketers
Branding removal and email previews are gated behind the paid Starter tier
Less suited to large-scale marketing campaign management compared to dedicated marketing automation platforms

faqs.question: What is Knock used for?
faqs.answer: Knock is used by engineering teams to build and manage product notifications, such as transactional emails, push alerts, SMS, in-app messages, and Slack notifications, through a single API and workflow builder instead of building delivery infrastructure from scratch.

faqs.question: Is Knock free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Knock has a free Developer plan that includes 10,000 messages, 500 guide active users, and 500 AI agent credits per month, with unlimited workflows and channels.

faqs.question: Who founded Knock?
faqs.answer: Knock was founded in January 2021 by Sam Seely and Chris Bell, former product and engineering leaders at Frame.io.

faqs.question: How much does Knock cost?
faqs.answer: Knock's Starter plan costs 250 dollars per month for 50,000 messages, and Enterprise pricing is custom and based on volume, with usage-based overage charges billed monthly.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Knock and Novu?
faqs.answer: Knock is a hosted, managed notification infrastructure service, while Novu is an open-source project that companies can self-host for free as an alternative, trading managed reliability for infrastructure control.

faqs.question: Does Knock support SMS and push notifications?
faqs.answer: Yes, Knock supports email, SMS, push notifications, in-app messages, chat, and Slack, all orchestrated through the same workflow engine.

use_cases.title: Product notification infrastructure
use_cases.description: Engineering teams replace custom-built notification code with Knock's API to send transactional and lifecycle messages across channels reliably.
use_cases.audience: Software engineering teams at B2B SaaS companies

use_cases.title: In-app messaging and guides
use_cases.description: Teams add targeted banners, modals, and notification inboxes to their product UI using Knock's prebuilt frontend components.
use_cases.audience: Product teams building onboarding and engagement flows

use_cases.title: Multi-tenant and object-based notifications
use_cases.description: Companies with complex data models, like project management or collaboration tools, use Knock's custom object subscriptions to notify the right users about shared documents, projects, or threads.
use_cases.audience: SaaS platforms with collaborative or multi-tenant features

features: Unlimited delivery channels and team members
Volume-based or monthly active recipient pricing
Shared Slack channel and priority support

enrichment.short_description: KnowledgeOwl is a knowledge base and documentation platform that helps teams create, organize, and publish help articles, manuals, and internal wikis.
enrichment.seo_title: KnowledgeOwl Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: KnowledgeOwl is a knowledge base builder for support docs and internal wikis. Compare its AI features, four pricing tiers, and alternatives like Document360.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: KnowledgeOwl is a knowledge base and documentation software company founded in 2015 and headquartered in Boulder, Colorado. The company operates as a small, independently owned business with roughly nine employees, running entirely on its own profits with no outside venture investment; it is a certified B Corporation and a member of 1 Percent for the Planet, positioning itself as an ethically and environmentally conscious vendor rather than a venture-backed growth-at-all-costs SaaS company. The product lets businesses create, organize, and publish knowledge bases for a range of use cases: external customer-facing help centers, internal employee wikis and SOPs, partner and reseller documentation, and product user manuals. Authors write and format content in a WYSIWYG editor, organize articles into customizable categories and navigation trees, and publish to a fully brandable site with custom domains, custom CSS/HTML theming, and white-labeled removal of any KnowledgeOwl branding on higher tiers. In recent years KnowledgeOwl has layered a suite of AI features across its plans, including an AI chatbot that answers reader questions using the knowledge base content, AI-powered search, AI-assisted article creation and summarization, and an AI style-guide reviewer that checks drafts against a company's writing standards before publishing. Administrative features include granular roles and permissions for managing who can author, edit, or publish content, SAML SSO and Salesforce SSO for enterprise identity management, HIPAA and GDPR compliance agreements for regulated industries, a developer API for programmatic access and integrations, and reader analytics that show which articles are viewed, searched for without results, or rated unhelpful. KnowledgeOwl also offers concierge-style onboarding uncommon among SaaS documentation tools: every paid plan includes recurring one-on-one calls with a KnowledgeOwl expert, ramping from monthly on the entry plan up to weekly on higher tiers, plus white-glove migration help to move content over from a previous knowledge base or wiki platform. Pricing is structured around four tiers, each of which includes one knowledge base and one author by default, with additional authors available for 25 dollars each and additional knowledge bases for 50 dollars each. The Basic plan costs 100 dollars per month and supports up to 1,000 articles, unlimited readers, monthly expert calls, the core AI feature set, and 45 days of retained analytics data. The Pro plan, promoted as the most popular option, costs 250 dollars per month, raises the article limit to 2,500, adds bi-weekly expert calls, developer API access, full custom domain and branding control, and extends analytics retention to 90 days with reader-level reporting. The Business plan costs 500 dollars per month, supports up to 5,000 articles, includes weekly expert calls, advanced security controls, custom roles and granular permissions, and 450 days of analytics retention. The Enterprise plan uses custom pricing and removes article and author limits entirely, adding dedicated account management, a custom SLA with a 99.9 percent uptime guarantee, and unlimited analytics history. All plans come with a 30-day free trial that does not require a credit card, along with SAML/Salesforce SSO options, HIPAA and GDPR agreements, and a baseline 99.5 percent uptime guarantee. KnowledgeOwl's target customers are mid-market and enterprise support, IT, HR, and product teams that need a dedicated, purpose-built documentation tool rather than repurposing a general wiki, and who value hands-on onboarding support over a fully self-serve product. Its main competitors include Document360, a venture-backed knowledge base platform with similar AI and analytics features aimed at a comparable market; Zendesk Guide, which bundles knowledge base functionality into Zendesk's broader customer support suite; Atlassian Confluence, a much more general-purpose team wiki often repurposed for documentation; and newer entrants like Slite and Guru, which lean more toward internal team knowledge management. KnowledgeOwl differentiates itself from these alternatives primarily through its high-touch, human-assisted onboarding model, its independent and bootstrapped ownership structure as opposed to VC-backed rivals like Document360, and its narrower, more focused feature set aimed specifically at knowledge base publishing rather than broader helpdesk or general team collaboration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: KnowledgeOwl is a knowledge base platform founded in 2015 and based in Boulder, Colorado. It is a small, independently owned, profitable company (a certified B Corporation) rather than a venture-backed startup, and it focuses squarely on helping teams create, organize, and publish documentation.

The product covers both external use cases, like customer-facing help centers, and internal ones, like employee wikis and SOPs, with a WYSIWYG editor, customizable categories, and fully brandable, custom-domain publishing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: KnowledgeOwl has added AI throughout the product: an AI chatbot for readers, AI-powered search, AI-assisted article drafting and summarization, and an AI style-guide reviewer. Administrative tooling includes granular roles and permissions, SAML and Salesforce SSO, HIPAA and GDPR compliance agreements, and a developer API.

A distinguishing feature is high-touch onboarding: every paid plan includes recurring one-on-one calls with a KnowledgeOwl expert, along with white-glove migration assistance when moving from another platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: KnowledgeOwl offers four tiers, each including one knowledge base and one author by default: Basic at 100 dollars/month (1,000 articles), Pro at 250 dollars/month (2,500 articles), Business at 500 dollars/month (5,000 articles), and custom-priced Enterprise with unlimited articles and a 99.9 percent uptime SLA.
Extra authors cost 25 dollars each and extra knowledge bases cost 50 dollars each. Every plan includes a 30-day free trial with no credit card required.

faqs.question: How much does KnowledgeOwl cost?
faqs.answer: KnowledgeOwl has four paid plans: Basic at 100 dollars per month, Pro at 250 dollars per month, Business at 500 dollars per month, and custom Enterprise pricing, all with a 30-day free trial.

faqs.question: Does KnowledgeOwl have a free plan?
faqs.answer: KnowledgeOwl does not offer a free-forever plan, but every paid tier includes a 30-day free trial with no credit card required.

faqs.question: When was KnowledgeOwl founded?
faqs.answer: KnowledgeOwl was founded in 2015 and is headquartered in Boulder, Colorado.

faqs.question: Does KnowledgeOwl have AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, KnowledgeOwl includes an AI chatbot, AI-powered search, AI-assisted article creation and summarization, and an AI style guide reviewer on all paid plans.

faqs.question: Is KnowledgeOwl good for internal wikis as well as customer help centers?
faqs.answer: Yes, KnowledgeOwl is commonly used for both external customer-facing help centers and internal employee wikis or documentation.

faqs.question: How is KnowledgeOwl different from Confluence?
faqs.answer: KnowledgeOwl is a purpose-built knowledge base publishing tool with reader analytics, branding, and AI search, while Confluence is a broader team wiki and collaboration tool often repurposed for documentation.

enrichment.short_description: Koala AI is an AI content writing platform that generates SEO-optimized, publish-ready articles using deep research, SERP analysis, and internal linking.
enrichment.seo_title: Koala AI Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Koala AI (koala.sh) is an AI SEO writing tool with Deep Research and SERP analysis. See KoalaWriter's pricing tiers and how it compares to Jasper and Copy.ai.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Koala AI, marketed at koala.sh, is an AI-powered content creation platform built for search-engine-optimized writing, launched with its flagship product KoalaWriter in April 2023 by founder Connor Smith. The company is based in Gainesville, Florida, and operates as a suite of five integrated tools sold under one subscription: KoalaWriter for long-form SEO articles, KoalaChat for conversational AI assistance, KoalaImages for AI-generated visuals, KoalaLinks for automated internal linking, and KoalaMagnets for lead-generation content. Koala AI reports serving more than 19,000 content creators, SEOs, publishers, agencies, and niche-site operators. Architecturally, KoalaWriter combines large language models, including GPT-5 and Claude 4 family models, with a Deep Research mode that pulls in live web data and citations to reduce hallucinated facts, and a real-time SERP analysis engine that scans top-ranking pages for a target keyword to surface the entities, subtopics, and semantic keywords an article needs to compete. Generated articles can be automatically formatted with schema markup, illustrated with AI-generated images, cross-linked to other pages on the same site through the automatic internal linking feature, and published directly to WordPress, Shopify, Webflow, or Ghost with formatting preserved. Additional capabilities include an outline editor for controlling article structure before generation, a Polish AI-editing pass aimed at improving readability, bulk content creation for producing many articles at once, and a specialized workflow for generating Amazon affiliate content using live product data. Pricing is usage-metered by word count and chat messages across nine subscription tiers, all offered with monthly or annual billing, annual billing saves roughly 20 percent, plus a free tier offering 5,000 words to try the product. The entry paid tier, Essentials, costs 9 dollars per month for 15,000 words and 250 chat messages with access to GPT and Claude models, AI images, and WordPress integration. The Professional tier costs 49 dollars per month for 100,000 words and 1,000 messages and adds unlimited uploads, automatic internal linking, and Deep Research. Higher tiers scale word and message allowances upward: Boost at 99 dollars for 250,000 words, Growth at 179 dollars for 500,000 words, Elite at 350 dollars for 1,000,000 words, and Advanced at 500 dollars for 1,500,000 words per month, with faster bulk-generation speeds unlocked at higher tiers. For agencies and large publishers, three Scale plans go from Scale I at 750 dollars per month for 2,500,000 words up to Scale III at 2,000 dollars per month for 10,000,000 words, with 3x faster bulk content creation. A notable pricing mechanic is that word allowances are calculated against GPT-5 Mini as the baseline model, and generating with the more capable GPT-5.2 or Claude 4.5 models consumes words at twice the rate. Koala AI targets solo bloggers, affiliate and niche-site publishers, SEO agencies managing multiple client sites, and in-house content marketing teams that need to produce large volumes of search-optimized articles faster than manual writing allows. Its most direct competitors are other AI SEO writing tools such as Content at Scale, Frase, and SurferSEO's AI writer, which similarly combine SERP research with content generation, as well as broader AI writing platforms like Jasper AI and Copy.ai that serve marketing copywriting more generally but lack Koala's SEO-specific SERP analysis and bulk-publishing workflows. Koala AI differentiates itself primarily through its Deep Research fact-checking layer, its all-in-one bundle of writing, image generation, internal linking, and lead-magnet tools under a single subscription, and pricing that scales granularly by word volume rather than a flat per-seat model, making it comparatively accessible for solo publishers at the low end while still offering enterprise-scale volume tiers for agencies producing tens of thousands of articles.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Koala AI, at koala.sh, is an AI writing platform focused specifically on search-optimized content. Launched in April 2023 by founder Connor Smith and based in Gainesville, Florida, it bundles five tools, KoalaWriter, KoalaChat, KoalaImages, KoalaLinks, and KoalaMagnets, under one subscription.

The platform reports over 19,000 users, ranging from solo bloggers to SEO agencies, and centers on generating publish-ready articles using GPT-5 and Claude 4 family models combined with live SERP research.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Koala AI's Deep Research mode pulls in live web data and citations to reduce hallucinated facts, while real-time SERP analysis surfaces the entities and semantic keywords needed to compete for a target keyword. Articles can include automatic schema markup, AI-generated images, and automatic internal linking.

Content publishes directly to WordPress, Shopify, Webflow, or Ghost with formatting preserved. Other tools include an outline editor, a readability-focused Polish pass, bulk content generation, and an Amazon affiliate article workflow using live product data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Koala AI offers a free tier with 5,000 words, then nine paid tiers from Essentials at 9 dollars per month (15,000 words) up to Scale III at 2,000 dollars per month (10,000,000 words), billed monthly or annually with a 20 percent annual discount.

Word allowances are calculated against GPT-5 Mini as the baseline; generating with the more capable GPT-5.2 or Claude 4.5 consumes words at twice the rate, and higher tiers unlock faster bulk content generation.

faqs.question: What is Koala AI used for?
faqs.answer: Koala AI, through its KoalaWriter tool, is used to generate SEO-optimized blog posts and articles using SERP research, fact-checked Deep Research, and automatic internal linking, then publish them directly to platforms like WordPress or Shopify.

faqs.question: Is Koala AI free?
faqs.answer: Koala AI offers a free tier with 5,000 words to try the platform, with paid plans starting at 9 dollars per month for 15,000 words.

faqs.question: Who created Koala AI?
faqs.answer: Koala AI's KoalaWriter product was created by founder Connor Smith and launched in April 2023.

faqs.question: What AI models does Koala AI use?
faqs.answer: Koala AI uses models from the GPT and Claude families, including GPT-5 and Claude 4, with word allowances calculated against GPT-5 Mini as the baseline.

faqs.question: Does Koala AI integrate with WordPress?
faqs.answer: Yes, Koala AI supports one-click publishing to WordPress, as well as Shopify, Webflow, and Ghost.

faqs.question: How is Koala AI different from Jasper or Copy.ai?
faqs.answer: Koala AI focuses specifically on SEO content with built-in SERP analysis, Deep Research fact-checking, and automatic internal linking, while Jasper and Copy.ai are broader marketing copywriting tools without the same SEO-research depth.

enrichment.short_description: Kodi is a free, open-source media center application that organizes and plays local and network video, music, photos, and live TV across many devices.
enrichment.seo_title: Kodi Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kodi is a free, open-source home theater software from the XBMC Foundation. See its features, supported platforms, and how it compares to Plex and Jellyfin.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kodi is a free, open-source home theater and media center application with roots dating back to 2002, when it began as an independently developed homebrew media player for the original Xbox console called Xbox Media Player. After merging with a similar project called YAMP in November 2002, the software was renamed Xbox Media Center, abbreviated XBMC, and its first stable release, XboxMediaCenter 1.0.0, shipped on June 29, 2004. To manage growing development costs, paperwork, and conference travel, the community established the XBMC Foundation as a tax-exempt, registered non-profit organization in the United States on January 16, 2009, and this foundation remains the legal entity that owns and governs Kodi's code today. In July 2014, with the release of version 14, the project was rebranded from XBMC to Kodi, reflecting its expansion well beyond the original Xbox platform, while the XBMC Foundation name was retained for the legal entity. Kodi is developed entirely by a global community of unpaid volunteers, more than 500 software developers have contributed code and over 100 translators have helped localize the interface into more than 70 languages, and the source code is distributed under the GNU General Public License version 2 or later. Functionally, Kodi is a highly customizable, skinnable application that organizes and plays local and network media: movies, TV shows, music, photos, podcasts, and live TV or DVR content accessed through PVR add-ons. It supports an extremely wide range of video and audio codecs and container formats, scrapes rich metadata such as posters, fan art, cast information, and plot summaries for movies and TV libraries, and can sync a shared media library across multiple installations using a central MySQL or MariaDB database. Kodi's add-on repository system is central to its appeal, letting users install additional skins, video and audio plugins, PVR backends for live TV tuners, and other extensions to customize functionality well beyond the base application. It runs natively on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, tvOS, and BSD, and is commonly deployed on dedicated home-theater PCs, Raspberry Pi boards, and set-top boxes, often via purpose-built Kodi-based Linux distributions such as LibreELEC and OSMC that turn low-cost hardware into a dedicated streaming appliance. Users can control playback through the on-device interface, a built-in web interface, or companion remote-control apps on phones and tablets. Because Kodi is entirely free and community-funded through donations to the XBMC Foundation, there is no subscription, license fee, or paid tier of any kind, and the project's continued existence does not depend on a commercial company remaining in business. Kodi's target audience is home theater PC enthusiasts, cord-cutters who want to organize and play their own media collections or watch live TV without a cable subscription, and DIY hobbyists who build custom streaming boxes on inexpensive hardware. Its main competitors and alternatives include Plex, a freemium, more consumer-polished media server that uses a client-server model with a central server streaming to multiple remote clients and offers paid premium features like remote access and live TV DVR; Jellyfin, a free and fully open-source Plex-style media server that, like Kodi, has no commercial company behind it and emphasizes privacy and community governance; and Emby, another freemium client-server media server comparable to Plex. Kodi differs from all three architecturally: rather than a client-server model with a central media server streaming to many remote devices, Kodi is traditionally installed and run directly on the playback device itself, though it can also browse and stream from network shares or, via add-ons, from an existing Plex or Jellyfin server, and it offers no official cloud relay service or commercial premium tier since the entire project remains volunteer-run and donation-funded.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kodi is a free, open-source media center application with origins going back to 2002 as Xbox Media Player, later renamed XBMC in 2004 and then Kodi in July 2014. It is developed by a global volunteer community and governed by the non-profit XBMC Foundation, established in 2009.

The software organizes and plays local and network video, music, photos, podcasts, and live TV, and runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, tvOS, and BSD, making it a favorite for home theater PCs and DIY streaming boxes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kodi supports an extensive range of audio and video formats, scrapes rich metadata for movie and TV libraries, and can sync a shared library across devices via a central MySQL or MariaDB database. Live TV and DVR functionality is available through PVR add-ons.

Its skinnable interface and large add-on repository let users heavily customize the experience, from new themes to streaming plugins and remote-control apps, and it is commonly paired with dedicated distributions like LibreELEC and OSMC on Raspberry Pi hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kodi is completely free with no subscription, license fee, or paid tier of any kind. It is funded through donations to the XBMC Foundation rather than commercial revenue.

Because there is no company selling the software, Kodi's continued availability does not depend on a business remaining profitable, distinguishing it from freemium alternatives like Plex and Emby that gate certain features behind paid plans.

features.title: Skinnable, fully customizable interface
features.description: Change themes and layouts through the add-on repository to personalize the entire experience.

features.title: Extensive add-on ecosystem
features.description: Install video and audio plugins, PVR backends, and skins to extend functionality well beyond the base app.

features.title: Broad format and codec support
features.description: Play a wide range of video and audio containers and codecs from local and network storage.

features.title: Live TV and DVR
features.description: Watch and record live television through PVR add-ons connected to tuner hardware or IPTV sources.

features.title: Cross-platform availability
features.description: Run Kodi on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, tvOS, and BSD.

features.title: Metadata scraping
features.description: Automatically fetch posters, fan art, cast info, and plot summaries for movie, TV, and music libraries.

features.title: Multi-device library sync
features.description: Share a single media library across multiple Kodi installations using a central MySQL or MariaDB database.

features.title: Remote control options
features.description: Control playback via the on-device interface, a built-in web interface, or companion remote apps.

pros: Completely free with no ads, subscriptions, or licensing fees
Fully open source under the GPL, auditable and modifiable
Massive add-on ecosystem for deep customization
Runs on almost any hardware and OS, including Raspberry Pi
No dependency on a company staying in business
cons: Steeper learning curve than consumer apps like Plex
No official remote-streaming or cloud access without third-party add-ons
Relies on volunteer community support rather than dedicated customer service
Some third-party add-ons raise legal and security concerns
Interface and setup can feel dated compared to modern streaming apps

faqs.question: Is Kodi free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kodi is completely free and open source, with no subscription fees, distributed under the GPL version 2 or later license.

faqs.question: Who develops Kodi?
faqs.answer: Kodi is developed by a global community of volunteer contributors and governed by the XBMC Foundation, a non-profit established in 2009.

faqs.question: What was Kodi called before?
faqs.answer: Kodi was originally released in 2004 as Xbox Media Center, or XBMC, before being renamed Kodi in July 2014.

faqs.question: Is Kodi legal?
faqs.answer: Kodi itself is fully legal open-source software; legal risk comes only from unofficial third-party add-ons that stream copyrighted content without authorization.

faqs.question: What devices does Kodi run on?
faqs.answer: Kodi runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, tvOS, and BSD, and is commonly installed on Raspberry Pi and dedicated home theater PC hardware.

faqs.question: How is Kodi different from Plex?
faqs.answer: Kodi is a standalone, local media player app you install directly on your playback device, while Plex uses a client-server model with a central media server and offers commercial premium features like remote streaming.

use_cases.title: Home theater PC media center

use_cases.title: Cord-cutter live TV and DVR

use_cases.title: Custom streaming device builds
use_cases.description: Hobbyists install Kodi-based distributions like LibreELEC or OSMC on small-form-factor devices to create dedicated streaming appliances.

features: Available on all major desktop and mobile platforms

enrichment.short_description: Koel is a self-hosted, open-source personal music streaming server that lets you stream your own music library from a private web app.
enrichment.seo_title: Koel Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Koel is an open-source, self-hosted music streaming server built with Laravel and Vue. Compare its free Community edition, Koel Plus pricing, and rivals.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Koel is a self-hosted personal music streaming server created by developer Phan An, with the project in active development since 2015. It is built with a Laravel (PHP) backend and a Vue.js frontend, describing itself as the music streaming solution that actually works, and its Community edition remains free and open source, distributed via a public GitHub repository at github.com/koel/koel with documentation at docs.koel.dev. Rather than a hosted SaaS product, Koel is software that users install on their own server, NAS, or VPS, typically with Docker, to stream a personal music collection through a modern, Spotify-like web interface without handing files or listening data to a third-party company. Core features available to all users include transparent lossless audio streaming, cross-device playback synchronization so a queue picks up where it left off on another device, favorites and smart playlists based on saved criteria, an equalizer, music visualizers, and support for radio stations and podcasts alongside a standard music library. A key architectural decision is that Koel speaks the Subsonic and OpenSubsonic APIs, the de facto open standard for self-hosted music servers, which means any existing Subsonic-compatible mobile or desktop client can connect to a Koel server, in addition to Koel's own native Koel Player apps for iOS and Android. Koel also integrates with external services including MusicBrainz, Wikipedia, Last.fm, Spotify, and YouTube to enrich library metadata and artist information, and it is optimized to remain fast even with very large music libraries, with multiple interface themes available. On top of the free Community edition, Koel offers a paid upgrade called Koel Plus that uses a one-time-purchase, pay-what-you-want pricing model starting at 19.99 euros, explicitly avoiding subscriptions, recurring payments, or per-seat costs. Koel Plus adds features aimed at more advanced or shared deployments: support for additional cloud storage drivers so a library is not limited to local disk, individual per-user libraries so multiple people can maintain separate collections on one shared server, collaboration and sharing tools, and single sign-on support. Because Koel has no venture backing or corporate ownership disclosed, its funding model is the direct one-time purchase of Koel Plus licenses plus voluntary community contribution, keeping the core Community product permanently free. Koel's target audience is self-hosting and homelab enthusiasts, privacy-conscious music collectors who want to retain full ownership of their audio files rather than relying on a streaming subscription, and technically capable users comfortable running a Docker container or PHP application on their own infrastructure. Its closest competitor is Navidrome, another free, open-source, self-hosted Subsonic-compatible music server, but written in Go rather than PHP, which some self-hosters prefer for its lower resource footprint and lack of any paid tier; other alternatives include Airsonic and Airsonic-Advanced, older Java-based Subsonic-API forks, and the broader general-purpose media servers Plex and Jellyfin, both of which support music streaming as one of several media types alongside video, rather than being purpose-built exclusively for music the way Koel is. Koel differentiates itself from Plex and Jellyfin through its music-first focus and lightweight, modern Vue-based interface, and from Navidrome primarily through its one-time-purchase Koel Plus tier that funds continued development without requiring a subscription, while both projects otherwise share the same open, Subsonic-compatible philosophy of giving users full control over their own music.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Koel is a self-hosted, open-source personal music streaming server created by developer Phan An and in active development since 2015. Built with Laravel on the backend and Vue.js on the frontend, it lets users stream their own music library through a private, Spotify-like web app running on their own server.

The Community edition is free forever, while an optional Koel Plus upgrade adds features for more advanced or shared setups. Koel is aimed at self-hosters and homelab users who want full ownership of their music rather than a subscription streaming service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Koel supports transparent lossless streaming, cross-device playback sync, smart playlists, an equalizer, visualizers, and radio and podcast playback. It speaks the Subsonic and OpenSubsonic APIs, so any existing Subsonic-compatible client can connect, alongside Koel's own native Koel Player apps for iOS and Android.
Metadata is enriched through integrations with MusicBrainz, Wikipedia, Last.fm, Spotify, and YouTube, and the interface offers multiple themes while staying fast even on large libraries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Koel Community edition is free and open source with no artificial limits. Koel Plus is a one-time, pay-what-you-want purchase starting at 19.99 euros rather than a subscription.

Koel Plus unlocks cloud storage drivers, individual per-user libraries, collaboration and sharing tools, and single sign-on, features aimed at multi-user households or more advanced self-hosted deployments.

Fully open source and self-hosted, giving complete data ownership
Subsonic API compatibility unlocks a large ecosystem of existing mobile clients
Koel Plus is a one-time purchase, not a subscription
Modern, fast interface built with Vue.js
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge (server, PHP, database, Docker)
No official cloud-hosted version for non-technical users
Multi-user and cloud storage features are locked behind the paid Plus tier
Smaller community and ecosystem than Plex or Jellyfin
Relies on a single primary maintainer, a common open-source project risk

faqs.question: Is Koel free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Koel Community edition is free and open source. Koel Plus, a one-time paid upgrade starting at 19.99 euros, adds premium features like cloud storage and multi-library support.

faqs.question: Who created Koel?
faqs.answer: Koel was created by developer Phan An and has been in active development since 2015.

faqs.question: Does Koel work with mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, Koel speaks the Subsonic and OpenSubsonic APIs, so it works with many existing Subsonic-compatible mobile apps, and also has its own native Koel Player app.

faqs.question: What technology is Koel built with?
faqs.answer: Koel is built with a Laravel (PHP) backend and a Vue.js frontend.

faqs.question: Can Koel stream lossless audio?
faqs.answer: Yes, Koel supports transparent lossless audio streaming.

faqs.question: How is Koel different from Navidrome?
faqs.answer: Koel and Navidrome are both free, open-source, self-hosted Subsonic-compatible music servers; Koel is built on PHP/Laravel with a one-time-purchase premium tier, while Navidrome is written in Go and remains entirely free.

use_cases.description: Users connect existing Subsonic-compatible mobile apps to their self-hosted Koel server for offline-capable mobile listening.

enrichment.short_description: Koha is a free, open-source integrated library system used by libraries worldwide to manage cataloging, circulation, patrons, and online public access catalogs.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted); paid managed hosting varies by vendor
enrichment.seo_title: Koha Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Koha is the world's first open-source library system, used by thousands of libraries worldwide. See its features, hosting costs, and how it compares to Evergreen.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Koha is a free, open-source integrated library system, or ILS, created in 1999 by Katipo Communications under contract for the Horowhenua Library Trust, a three-branch public library system in New Zealand that needed to replace a legacy system which was not compliant with the year 2000 date change. The first live installation went into production in January 2000, with Chris Cormack, then employed at Katipo, serving as lead developer and remaining an active contributor to the project for decades afterward. Once the system was completed, Katipo and the Horowhenua Library Trust made the decision to release the code as open source rather than keep it proprietary, and the project was named Koha, which means gift or donation in the Maori language of New Zealand's indigenous population, reflecting that choice. Koha is widely credited as the first open-source integrated library system in the world, and it is governed today by a global community of contributors under formal structures that include the Horowhenua Library Trust Koha Committee and a community Code of Conduct, with the code released under the GNU General Public License. As of recent counts, more than 4,700 libraries across over 6,600 individual facilities and branches in more than 100 countries run Koha, spanning public, academic, school, and special libraries, a scale of adoption that has led industry analysts to describe it as the most widely implemented open-source ILS in the world. Functionally, Koha is a fully featured, web-based system covering the core operational needs of a library: cataloging using the MARC21 and UNIMARC bibliographic standards, circulation management for checkouts, holds, and fines, acquisitions and serials management for ordering and tracking new materials and periodicals, patron and member account management, and a public-facing Online Public Access Catalog, or OPAC, that lets patrons search the collection, place holds, and manage their own accounts through a responsive interface that works across desktop and mobile devices. Because Koha carries no licensing fee and its source code is fully open, libraries have two practical paths to running it: self-hosting the software themselves on infrastructure running MySQL or MariaDB with a Perl-based application layer and a search index such as Zebra or Elasticsearch, which requires in-house technical staff, or contracting one of several independent commercial support companies, including firms such as ByWater Solutions, PTFS Europe, and BibLibre, for managed hosting, data migration, customization, and ongoing support. Because the Koha project itself does not sell hosting or support contracts, pricing for the managed-hosting path is set independently by each vendor and varies by library size and service level rather than being fixed centrally, meaning total cost of ownership for adopting libraries typically comes down to either staff time for self-hosting or a support contract fee paid to a chosen vendor. Development is sponsored collectively by libraries of varying types and sizes, individual volunteers, and these commercial support companies, with releases coordinated by elected release managers, community mailing lists and chat channels, and an annual KohaCon conference that brings the global community together. Koha's target users are public libraries, school libraries, academic and special libraries, and multi-branch library consortia, particularly smaller or budget-constrained institutions that want to avoid the recurring licensing costs of proprietary commercial ILS platforms while still getting enterprise-grade cataloging and circulation functionality comparable to paid alternatives. Its principal competitors are Evergreen, another prominent free and open-source ILS originally built for the Georgia Public Library Service and especially popular with large library consortia, and proprietary commercial systems such as Ex Libris Alma and Voyager, SirsiDynix Symphony, Follett Destiny, which is common in K-12 school libraries, and Innovative Interfaces' Polaris and Sierra platforms. Koha differentiates itself from Evergreen through its longer history dating back to 1999 versus Evergreen's 2006 origin, its broader overall global adoption footprint, and its larger ecosystem of competing commercial support vendors; compared with proprietary systems like SirsiDynix or Ex Libris, Koha's core differentiator is that adopting libraries own their data and code outright with zero licensing fees and can switch support vendors freely at any time, whereas proprietary ILS platforms lock a library into a single vendor's pricing structure and product roadmap.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Koha is a free, open-source integrated library system created in 1999 by Katipo Communications for the Horowhenua Library Trust in New Zealand, with its first live installation going into production in January 2000. Widely credited as the first open-source ILS in the world, its name means gift in Maori, reflecting the decision to release it as open source.

Today Koha is used by more than 4,700 libraries across over 6,600 facilities in more than 100 countries, spanning public, academic, school, and special libraries, governed by a global volunteer community and released under the GPL.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Koha covers the core operational needs of a library: MARC21 and UNIMARC cataloging, circulation management for checkouts, holds and fines, acquisitions and serials management, and patron account administration, all through a responsive web interface.

A public-facing OPAC lets patrons search the collection, place holds, and manage their own accounts. Multi-branch and consortium support lets groups of libraries share a single installation and let patrons borrow across branches.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Koha carries no licensing fee since it is fully open source, so libraries can self-host it on their own infrastructure for free, requiring in-house technical staff with Perl and MARC expertise.

Alternatively, libraries can contract independent commercial vendors such as ByWater Solutions, PTFS Europe, or BibLibre for managed hosting, migration, and support, with pricing set individually by each vendor rather than by the Koha project itself.

features.description: Maintain patron accounts, borrowing history, and permissions.

features.description: Let patrons search the catalog, place holds, and manage their own accounts online.

features.title: Acquisitions and serials management
features.description: Order and track new materials and periodical subscriptions.

features.title: Multi-branch and consortium support
features.description: Share a single Koha installation across multiple library branches or a regional consortium.

features.title: Responsive web design
features.description: Access staff and patron interfaces from desktop and mobile devices.

features.title: Community-driven development
features.description: Benefit from continuous improvements contributed by libraries, volunteers, and commercial support vendors worldwide, with no licensing fees.

pros: Completely free and open source with no per-seat licensing costs
Most widely adopted open-source ILS, used by thousands of libraries globally
Full data ownership with no vendor lock-in
Mature project with over two decades of continuous development
Multiple competing commercial support vendors to choose from
cons: Requires technical staff or a paid support vendor to self-host and maintain
Interface can feel dated compared to some modern proprietary systems
Community support relies on mailing lists and forums rather than guaranteed SLAs
Customization and migration typically require Perl and MARC expertise
Support and hosting costs vary widely by vendor, making budgeting less predictable than fixed licensing

faqs.question: Is Koha free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Koha is free and open-source software with no licensing fees; libraries only pay if they choose a commercial vendor for hosting, migration, or support.

faqs.question: Who created Koha?
faqs.answer: Koha was created in 1999 by Katipo Communications for the Horowhenua Library Trust in New Zealand, with the first installation going live in January 2000.

faqs.question: What does Koha mean?
faqs.answer: Koha means gift or donation in the Maori language, reflecting the decision to release the software as open source.

faqs.question: How many libraries use Koha?
faqs.answer: More than 4,700 libraries across over 6,600 facilities in over 100 countries run Koha.

faqs.question: Who provides support for Koha?
faqs.answer: Since Koha is community-developed, support and hosting are offered by third-party companies such as ByWater Solutions, PTFS Europe, and BibLibre, rather than by a single vendor.

faqs.question: How does Koha compare to Evergreen?
faqs.answer: Both are free, open-source library systems; Koha dates back to 1999 and has broader overall adoption, while Evergreen originated in 2006 and is especially popular with large library consortia.

use_cases.title: Public library cataloging and circulation

use_cases.title: School and academic library management

use_cases.title: Multi-branch consortium sharing
use_cases.description: Groups of libraries share a single Koha installation to pool cataloging effort and let patrons borrow across branches.
use_cases.audience: Library consortia and regional library networks

features: Full ILS functionality: cataloging, circulation, acquisitions, OPAC
Community support via mailing lists and wiki

enrichment.short_description: Koillection is a free, open-source, self-hosted web app for cataloging and organizing personal collections of any kind, from books to stamps to games.
enrichment.seo_title: Koillection Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Koillection is a free, self-hosted, open-source app for cataloging any type of collection. See its features, tech stack, and how it compares to CLZ Collectorz.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Koillection is a self-hosted web application for cataloging personal collections, created by developer Benjamin Jonard, with its public GitHub repository first created in May 2018 and released under the permissive MIT license as free, open-source software. It is designed for hobbyist collectors who want a dedicated system to organize and track physical collections of essentially any type, working conceptually like a nested file and directory system: users create top-level collections, for example books, stamps, vinyl records, action figures, or trading cards, and then add individual items inside them, each with custom fields, images, notes, and metadata. Technically, Koillection is built on the Symfony PHP framework combined with API Platform to provide a full REST API backend, paired with a Vue.js-driven frontend, and it functions as a Progressive Web App, meaning it can be installed on a phone or tablet home screen and used much like a native mobile app, including offline-capable behavior. Self-hosting the application requires a PHP environment together with a database of MySQL 8.0 or later, MariaDB 10.0 or later, or PostgreSQL 10.0 or later, and the project provides official Docker images to simplify deployment on home servers, NAS devices, or VPS instances, making it a common addition to homelab and self-hosted-app stacks. A deliberate design choice is that Koillection does not ship with built-in metadata scrapers for specific catalogs, such as automatic ISBN lookups for books or barcode databases for other collectibles; instead it lets users build their own custom scrapers against its REST API or enter metadata manually, trading some out-of-the-box convenience for flexibility and a lighter core application. Feature-wise, Koillection supports an unlimited number of collections and nested sub-collections, rich custom item-level metadata fields including text, numbers, images, and choice lists, tagging that works across different collections so related items can be grouped regardless of which collection they belong to, wishlists for tracking desired items, loan and missing-item tracking through a signs system, a night and dark mode theme, multi-user accounts with independent private collections per user, and granular sharing controls that let a user expose specific collections publicly or to specific people via shareable links. Because the application runs entirely on infrastructure the user controls, all data, images, and metadata remain on the user's own server rather than a third-party company's cloud, which is a core selling point for privacy-conscious collectors who do not want an inventory of potentially valuable items sitting on a commercial platform's servers. There is no company, subscription, hosted SaaS offering, or paid tier behind Koillection: it is maintained as a community open-source project on GitHub, with user-interface translations crowdsourced through the Crowdin platform, and every user self-hosts and maintains their own independent instance for free. Koillection's target audience is hobbyist collectors across categories like books, comics, stamps, coins, toys, and trading cards, home archivists cataloging a personal media library, and self-hosting or homelab enthusiasts who already run a Docker-based home server and want a dedicated, private inventory application rather than relying on spreadsheets, generic note-taking apps, or a commercial SaaS cataloging tool. Its closest competitors are the CLZ, or Collectorz.com, family of cataloging applications, including Book Collector, Comic Collector, and Movie Collector, which are commercial, per-category desktop and cloud products that include built-in barcode scanning and metadata lookups but charge licensing fees and are closed source; Delicious Library, a paid macOS-only cataloging application; and general-purpose productivity tools like Notion or Airtable, which some collectors repurpose as makeshift collection databases despite lacking a purpose-built collection, item, and tag data model. Koillection differentiates itself from the CLZ product family primarily by being entirely free, self-hosted, and general-purpose across any type of collectible rather than sold per-category with recurring or one-time licensing fees, while trading away CLZ's built-in barcode scanning and commercial metadata databases in exchange for complete control over one's own server, data, and privacy.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Koillection is a free, self-hosted web app for cataloging personal collections, created by developer Benjamin Jonard, with its GitHub repository dating to May 2018 and released under the MIT license. It works like a nested file system: users create collections, such as books, stamps, or trading cards, then add items inside them with custom metadata.

Built with Symfony and API Platform on the backend and Vue.js on the frontend, Koillection is also a Progressive Web App, letting it be installed on phones and tablets like a native app while running entirely on infrastructure the user controls.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Koillection supports unlimited collections and nested sub-collections, rich custom item fields, cross-collection tagging, wishlists, and a loan or missing-item tracking system called signs. Multi-user accounts let several people maintain independent private collections on one instance.

Sharing controls let users expose specific collections publicly or privately via shareable links, and a full REST API supports custom integrations and scrapers, since Koillection deliberately ships without built-in metadata lookups like ISBN or barcode databases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Koillection is completely free and open source, with no subscription, paid tier, or hosted SaaS offering from the project itself.

Users self-host the application via Docker on their own server, NAS, or VPS, requiring a PHP environment and a MySQL, MariaDB, or PostgreSQL database, with all costs limited to the user's own hosting infrastructure.

features.description: Share specific collections publicly or with specific people through shareable links.

features.title: Progressive Web App support
features.description: Install Koillection on a phone or tablet home screen for a native-app-like experience.

Full data ownership through self-hosting
Works for literally any type of collection, not just one category
Progressive Web App support gives a native-app-like mobile experience
REST API allows custom scrapers and integrations
cons: Requires self-hosting skills such as Docker, PHP, and database setup
No built-in metadata lookups like ISBN or barcode scanning
No official hosted cloud version for non-technical users
Smaller community than mainstream self-hosted apps
Relies on a single primary maintainer for long-term development

faqs.question: Is Koillection free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Koillection is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host Koillection?
faqs.answer: Yes, Koillection is a self-hosted application; there is no official hosted cloud version, so you run it on your own server, NAS, or VPS, typically via Docker.

faqs.question: What can I catalog with Koillection?
faqs.answer: Koillection is designed to catalog any kind of physical collection, including books, stamps, coins, vinyl records, trading cards, and action figures.

faqs.question: What technology does Koillection use?
faqs.answer: Koillection is built with the Symfony PHP framework and API Platform on the backend and Vue.js on the frontend, and supports MySQL, MariaDB, or PostgreSQL.

faqs.question: Does Koillection have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Koillection is a Progressive Web App, so it can be installed on phones and tablets and used like a native app without a separate app-store download.

faqs.question: Can I share my collection with others using Koillection?
faqs.answer: Yes, Koillection supports public and private sharing links so you can expose specific collections to other people.

use_cases.title: Hobbyist collection cataloging
use_cases.description: Collectors of stamps, coins, comics, or toys self-host Koillection to track their full inventory with photos and custom metadata.

use_cases.title: Home library and media inventory
use_cases.description: Book and media owners use Koillection to catalog their physical libraries and track loans to friends and family.

use_cases.title: Homelab self-hosted inventory app
use_cases.description: Self-hosting enthusiasts add Koillection to their Docker-based home server stack alongside other privacy-focused apps.

enrichment.short_description: Komga is a free, open-source, self-hosted media server for organizing and reading comics, manga, magazines, and eBooks from any device.
enrichment.seo_title: Komga Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Komga is a free, open-source, self-hosted comics and manga server with OPDS and Kobo Sync support. See its features and how it compares to Kavita and Plex.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Komga is a free, open-source, self-hosted media server built specifically for comics, manga, French-language BDs (bandes dessinees), magazines, and eBooks, created by developer Gauthier Roebroeck, known on GitHub as gotson, with the project's public repository dating back to 2019. Written in Kotlin and distributed under the MIT license, Komga is packaged as a Docker-friendly Java application that users run on their own server, NAS, or home lab hardware rather than through any hosted cloud service. The server ingests files in CBZ, CBR, PDF, and EPUB formats and organizes them into libraries, collections, and reading lists, while allowing users to view and edit metadata such as series titles, publishers, genres, age ratings, and reading order directly within the app. Access is provided through several complementary channels: a responsive, built-in web reader that works across desktop, tablet, and phone browsers without installing anything; compatibility with the OPDS and OPDS-PS (Page Streaming) syndication protocols, which lets any OPDS-compatible e-reader or reading app browse and download from a Komga library; native Kobo Sync support, which lets Kobo e-readers pull new books directly from a Komga server without manual file transfers; KOReader Sync, which keeps reading progress synchronized with the popular KOReader app; and compatibility with popular third-party reading apps such as Mihon (the actively maintained successor to Tachiyomi) on Android. A full REST API underlies the entire server, which has enabled a community ecosystem of companion scripts and tools for automation, metadata scraping, and library management beyond what ships in the core application. Komga supports multi-user access with per-library permission controls, including age-restriction and content-label settings, which makes it practical for households sharing one server while limiting what younger users can browse or read. There is no company or commercial entity behind Komga: it is maintained by its creator together with community contributors, and the project accepts informal donations rather than charging any subscription fee, meaning there is no paid tier, hosted SaaS version, or licensing cost of any kind. Project documentation is published through a Docusaurus-based site at komga.org, and an active Discord server serves as the primary hub for user support, feature requests, and community discussion. Komga's target users are comic and manga readers with substantial digital collections, digital library managers, and self-hosting hobbyists who want a centralized, cross-device reading experience comparable to what commercial digital comics services have historically offered, but running entirely under their own control and without a recurring subscription. Its closest direct competitor is Kavita, another free, open-source, self-hosted comics and eBook server with a broadly similar feature set, including a web reader, OPDS support, and metadata management, and the choice between the two often comes down to interface preference and specific integrations, since both are actively maintained MIT-licensed community projects with comparable scope. Other adjacent alternatives include Ubooquity, an older and simpler self-hosted comic and eBook server; Calibre-Web, a self-hosted web interface built around the Calibre eBook library manager that is more eBook-focused than comics-focused; and the general-purpose media servers Plex and Jellyfin, both of which have added or support comic-reading capabilities through plugins or agents but were not purpose-built for sequential-art formats the way Komga was from the outset. Komga differentiates itself through comics-specific features that few competitors match, particularly its native Kobo Sync and KOReader Sync integrations, its full OPDS compliance for broad e-reader app compatibility, and its lightweight, Docker-first deployment model that targets self-hosters specifically rather than the broader mainstream consumer audience that Plex and Jellyfin also serve with their all-purpose media libraries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Komga is a free, open-source, self-hosted media server built specifically for comics, manga, and eBooks, created by developer Gauthier Roebroeck with the project dating back to 2019 and released under the MIT license. Written in Kotlin and distributed as a Docker-friendly Java application, it runs entirely on infrastructure the user controls.
Komga ingests CBZ, CBR, PDF, and EPUB files and organizes them into libraries, collections, and reading lists, with metadata editing for series titles, publishers, genres, and reading order.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Access is provided through a built-in responsive web reader, OPDS and OPDS-PS syndication support for any compatible e-reader app, native Kobo Sync for direct Kobo device access, KOReader Sync for progress synchronization, and compatibility with third-party apps like Mihon on Android.
A full REST API underlies the server, enabling community automation tools, and multi-user access with age and content-label restrictions makes Komga practical for households sharing a single library.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Komga is completely free and open source with no subscription, licensing fee, or paid tier of any kind, funded informally through community donations rather than commercial revenue.

There is no official hosted cloud version; users self-host Komga on their own server, NAS, or home lab hardware, typically via Docker, and support is available through an active community Discord server.

features.title: CBZ, CBR, PDF, and EPUB support
features.description: Organize and read comics, manga, and eBooks across multiple common file formats in one library.

features.title: Built-in responsive web reader
features.description: Read directly in the browser on desktop, tablet, or phone without installing any additional software.

features.title: OPDS and OPDS-PS syndication support
features.description: Connect any OPDS-compatible e-reader app to browse and download from your Komga library.

features.title: Kobo Sync and KOReader Sync
features.description: Sync new books and reading progress directly to Kobo e-readers and the KOReader app.

features.title: Multi-user access with content restrictions
features.description: Set per-library permissions, age restrictions, and content labels for shared household use.

features.title: REST API for automation
features.description: Build custom scripts and integrations against Komga's full REST API.

features.title: Metadata editing
features.description: View and edit series and book metadata including titles, publishers, genres, and reading order.

features.title: Docker-friendly self-hosted deployment
features.description: Run Komga on your own server or NAS hardware using official Docker images.

pros: Completely free and open source with no subscription
Purpose-built for comics and manga, not a generic media server
Wide compatibility through OPDS and third-party reader apps like Mihon
Kobo Sync and KOReader Sync are rare, high-value features for e-reader owners
Active Discord community and regular updates
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge and server infrastructure
No official hosted cloud version
No built-in remote-access relay service like Plex offers
Smaller mainstream awareness compared to Plex or Jellyfin
Depends on a single lead maintainer for long-term project continuity

faqs.question: Is Komga free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Komga is completely free and open-source software under the MIT license, with no subscription or paid tier.

faqs.question: What file formats does Komga support?
faqs.answer: Komga supports CBZ, CBR, PDF, and EPUB files for comics, manga, and eBooks.

faqs.question: Can I read Komga on a Kobo e-reader?
faqs.answer: Yes, Komga has native Kobo Sync support, letting Kobo e-readers pull books directly from your Komga server.

faqs.question: Who created Komga?
faqs.answer: Komga was created by developer Gauthier Roebroeck, with the project beginning in 2019.

faqs.question: Does Komga have a mobile reading app?
faqs.answer: Komga does not ship its own dedicated mobile app, but it works with third-party apps like Mihon on Android and any OPDS-compatible e-reader app.

faqs.question: How is Komga different from Kavita?
faqs.answer: Komga and Kavita are both free, open-source, self-hosted comic and eBook servers with similar core features; they differ mainly in UI design and specific integrations like Komga's Kobo Sync support.

use_cases.title: Personal comics and manga library
use_cases.description: Readers with large digital comics or manga collections self-host Komga to browse and read from any device with a consistent, centralized library.

use_cases.title: Kobo and e-reader synchronization
use_cases.description: Kobo e-reader owners connect their device directly to a Komga server to sync new comics and reading progress without manual file transfers.

use_cases.title: Family media server with content restrictions
use_cases.description: Households set up multi-user access with age and content-label restrictions so a shared Komga library is safe for younger readers.
use_cases.audience: Families sharing a home media server

features: Self-hosted comics and eBook media server
Support for CBZ, CBR, PDF, EPUB
OPDS, Kobo Sync, and KOReader Sync
